sentences.text: Well, the first name is C-E-L-I-N-A.
My last name is B like in boy, I-N-I-A-Z.
Karp, Celina Karp, K-A-R-P.
I was born in Krakow, Poland.
On the 28th of May, 1931.
In Poland, it was Ignatzi Karp and Felicia Karp.
No, I did not.
I was an only child.
My father was an accountant.
She was also an accountant.
I had a very nice, happy childhood.
Lived sort of a middle class existence.
My parents both worked-- my mother was a professional person, so we had a governess who took care of me.
It was important to be-- to behave in a certain way.
I was always very well dressed, always wore white gloves, even to play in the sandbox in the park.
Life was kind of nice till the war.
Only an aunt.
My mother's younger sister lived with us.
She was an artist.
She painted-- did lots of interesting embroidery.
Gucia.
Wittenburg.
It was my mother's middle name-- maiden name.
I would say that it would compare to the conservative Judaism in the United States.
We weren't really orthodox, that orthodox.
But then, I think, in Poland, there were no divisions in Judaism.
There was just one religion.
It was very mixed.
I would say it was predominantly non-Jewish.
You know, there were some Jewish families in the building, but I would say that, if I had to give it a percentage, I would say 75% non-Jewish, maybe 25% Jewish.
Yes, of course.
I went to a public school.
And in the public school, we were all mixed.
Well, I only went to two grades.
So what I remember is mostly first grade and second grade.
I remember my first grade teacher's name.
And it was Mrs.
Jablonska, which meant apple.
I enjoyed it tremendously.
I loved learning.
I remember saying to my mother, I will never learn how to read.
But I did.
And I-- I did enjoy reading tremendously.
In Poland, the libraries were not free, and you had to pay for checking out books.
And we used to do this continually.
I enjoyed that very much.
I had a lot of books.
That was my favorite gift.
And everybody knew that I enjoyed getting books as gifts.
Oh, I remember by Johanna Spyri.
And I think that stands out very much in my mind, because I kind of wished I had been Heidi and had that experience.
I remember reading James Fenimore Cooper, you know, I couldn't imagine what it was like to be a Native American, or an Indian in those days.
Adventures, Western stories, and things like that.
Well, I remember particularly, at the beginning-- you know, just prior to the war, and at the beginning of the war, there was a heightened feeling of something is going to happen.

And people used to congregate in the courtyard of the apartment building and talk about war, and feeling that if something-- if things were going to happen, things weren't going to be so good for the Jews.
But as a child, no.
I don't think that I felt any anti-Semitism.
I really wouldn't have known what that was.
I remember everybody talking, congregating, and-- and speaking about it.
And there was a feeling of not understanding what was happening, but feeling that something was ominous, because everybody was concerned.
You know, the older people were concerned and talked in hushed tones, and listened to the foreign broadcasts on the Telefunken radio-- on the shortwave.
Oh, I-- it just-- a fear.
I think it was just a general fear, and is-- was our life going to change?
That sort of thing.
And-- and a feeling of-- we couldn't do certain things.
And then when war broke out, of course, right away, the schools were closed, and so our lives really changed.
Well, I think as soon as-- as soon as war broke out, you know, and we heard-- well, bombs started falling-- there were like three, I think, fell in Krakow on the radio station.
I had just received, just prior to that, a puppy, which was wonderful.
My-- my aunt bought it for me.
It was a little spitz, white spitz.
And one of the first things that I had to part with, painfully, was to give up my dog to a shelter, because my parents felt that, couldn't take care of it with the war approaching.
And I distinctly remember having to take him to a shelter.
And that was painful, because I didn't understand why, you know-- I was only about eight years old at the time.
I remember people panicking.
There was a general fear.

I remember that the neighbors that discussed the impending problems in the courtyard on the 2nd of September-- I mean, war started on the 1st September-- on the 2nd of September, appeared with armbands sporting swastikas.
They turned out to have been fifth columnists.

So it was really kind of an incredible feeling to realize that you've been trusting people and-- an telling them your feelings and your attitudes, and they were sympathizers of the Nazis, or of the Germans at the time, you know.
I don't think we really fully realized what Nazism was till it hit us later.
At that time, we just talked about Germany invading Poland.
Well, how did your life change when the war started?
Well, as I say, I lost my dog right away.
School closed, so we were not allowed to go to school.
We were sent-- for a very short time, some of the Jewish families organized a Jewish school, and we were sent there for a few-- just a few weeks, really.
And my father, of course, left right away.
I mean, all the men, as soon as war broke out, all the men went east, you know, on a long march, running-- sort of running away.
So we were just left-- all the women were left in-- in the building.
Then my father came back, fortunately.
But there was this incredible fear, you know, not knowing what was happening to my father.
And I could sense that, I could feel it, I could see it in my mother.
That was the beginning of it.
It was just kind of a totally unsettled life.
And by the time the winter came, my father was back-- after a long walk, you know, back.
Then when the winter came, right away, we were told to, you know, we were told to go out and shovel snow, and do all kinds of menial tasks for the Germans.
Life changed completely, right away.
My parents-- we knew that we were going to be-- there were rumors that we were going to be resettled into a ghetto.
So my parents got rid of their nice furniture and silver and china and all of these things, sold them to the various non-Jewish neighbors.
And I think that-- my mother-- I think it hurt my mother terribly, because she liked those things.
She was proud of them.
They were her trousseau.
It had taken them a while to accumulate these things, you know.
And it just-- life just changed, completely, you know.
You lost your sense-- not that you ever had a tremendous sense of security, but as a child, I did.
And at that point, I no longer had that sense of security.
It was totally replaced by the beginnings of fear, you know.
I remember my-- my father worked for a-- he was an accountant in a shirt factory named HoGo.
I think, the initials stood for Horowitz and Goldfarb, or something like that.
But it was sort of analogous to Arrow factory, Arrow Shirt, a Manhattan, here.
And he got one of the maintenance men there to come with a push cart-- you know that they used to carry the shirts from one place to another.
And we loaded up some of our personal belongings, and we moved-- I mean, we didn't-- a few sticks of furniture, like mattresses and beds.
And we moved.
Books, probably.
And maybe some dolls.
I was very fond of dolls.
We moved to-- well, they moved us-- the ghetto then became-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they moved us into what used to be the Jewish section of town.
And then, actually, they moved us twice.
You know, the ghetto kept shrinking.
So at first, it was much larger, and then it shrank some more and shrank some more.
And then they started breaking us up.

You know, they-- at first, we didn't have any walls around the ghetto, but then they started putting up a wall around the ghetto, and they even bricked in windows and doors, you know, of buildings facing towards the outside of the ghetto.
We, at first, lived in one of those buildings that faced to the outside of the ghetto-- so we could see some of the trees and things.

But then they moved us out of that house, because that house then became part of the outside of the ghetto, into more inner-- say, I think we moved twice, two or three times within the ghetto-- each time to a smaller accommodation.
Each time to share with more people.
In other words, you would have one room, and there would be another family in another room, and everybody would be sharing the kitchen and the hallway and the bathrooms.
Life just became totally different, you know.
Well, we still had-- well, right away, as I remember, they closed all the bank accounts, you know, Jewish bank accounts were closed.
So my parents couldn't withdraw any money.
But I'm sure that knowing the war was coming, I think everybody sort of put some money away, and some.
And I think they sold a few valuables things.
I mean, they got some money for the furniture, and-- I assume.
You know, I mean, I didn't question them at the time.
But now in retrospect, I would imagine that that's where the money came from and that that's how we paid for food, you know?
I mean-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews tend to be survivors.
What else do you remember about the ghetto?
Well, I remember-- you know, as a child in the ghetto, at first, it was not-- for a child, we were then squished together in a tight place, but there were lots of other children.
So in a sense, there was more time to play.
We didn't have school, so there was more time to play, more time to visit with other people.
At first, it wasn't so bad, you know?
I mean, for a child.
For the grown ups, it must have been terrible.
You know, when I think about it, now that I'm a parent, it must have been hard.
Did your parents work at that time?
Yes, they did.
They continued working.
Both my mother then, at that point, joined-- the HoGo factory was taken over, was given to a person named Madritsch.
And it became slowly turned from making shirts into making army uniforms for the Wehrmacht.
It's sort of a parallel to Schindler making-- you know, enameled pots and pans, then becoming-- making mess kits.
Madritsch then took over, and then my mother and my father both worked for Madritsch in the-- in the office.
My father ran the factory, because he sort of knew it-- he was, you know, in terms of accounting and being able to predict how to make money out of it, I guess.
And my mother worked in the office taking care of the books.
Just sort of hung around, really.
I was all alone, really.
They left the ghetto to go to work, and I was left behind.
Which, I'm sure, must have been very difficult for my parents.

And then, as things progressed and got worse and worse in the ghetto-- as they, you know, made the ghetto smaller by transporting people out of the ghetto, and at that point, we didn't know where they were transporting them, you know, except someplace.
As the ghetto got smaller and smaller, it became evident that-- at least for my parents-- that the best thing would be for me to go out with them to work.
So they were able to, through knowing somebody, to get a blue card for me, which was a permit-- a work permit, really, to go out of the ghetto.
For a while there, I worked in the ghetto.
I worked at a paper cooperative.
They only employed children, really-- licking envelopes, making envelopes.
I also worked at a brush co-operative making brushes, sticking, you know, the bristles into a brush and pulling it with a wire.
But that was not very long that I remember.
My parents were able to get-- to fake my age, make it two years older, to allow me to go out of the ghetto with them.
And got the blue card for me, and then I joined them going to Madritsch out of the ghetto.
And I was-- then started working on a sewing machine.
Sewed uniforms, exactly sewed uniforms.
You know what piece work is?
That's what I did.
You know, everybody gets either a piece of a sleeve or a piece of a pocket, and-- and you work on it.
Well, let me see.
I must have been nine-- nine or 10, and they made me two years older.
So it would have been, you know, according to the records, 12.
Between nine and 10.
Must have been 1940 or '41, someplace in between there.
Probably right about there, you know.

I'm not exact on the figures, because I don't really remember the exact times that the ghetto got smaller and smaller, except that I think in 1943, it closed completely, and we were transported to-- that was the last selection.
We were transported to Plaszow, which became a labor camp.
At first, it was a labor camp.
Then it became a concentration camp.

What I mean by that is that, one day they came in, and I think-- I think this is what is shown so very well in because-- except that I don't think-- rightly so, it was not shown in its complete horror.
Because what really happened is that the German-- well, I don't know whether they were SS or soldiers or whoever-- took some of the children and absolutely knocked their heads on the walls.
They swung them by the legs and killed them that way.
It was a horrible experience.
I saw it, yes.
We all saw it, you know.
I was afraid.
I mean, what did I think?
The overwhelming emotion that has probably ruled my life has been fear-- fear of authority and fear in general.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
OK.
It doesn't want to.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
As I said, you know, the overwhelming emotion that I remember was fear.
You know-- not so much anger, but kind of a questioning, how could anybody do anything like that?

I think it was at the age of 12 that I know that I lost my faith in God, because I knew that no-- I mean, the Jews believed that God-- at least the explanation was-- that the reason things were happening to the Jews because of the sins of our forefathers.
And I simply couldn't believe that any god would allow a child to be killed.
So I had-- I had my doubts about it, and I have not really resolved them since.
Babies.
Well, we-- we went-- we were transported out of the ghetto and into Plaszow.
I believe they took us by trucks-- or we walked.
I can't even remember.
We might have walked directly from the factory.
I'm not sure.
Of course, at that point, everything was left behind.
The only thing that we had on were the clothes on our backs, you know.
So no possessions at all.
We, you know, we lived in barracks, five to a berth.
That was it.
But for a while there, you know, we were able to walk out of the Plaszow camp and walk about-- I think the trip took about 90 minutes each way to our-- to the factory, to Madritsch factory.
The factory was moved really from-- the factory had been moved originally, I think, from middle of Krakow to Podgorze, which is kind of a little suburb over the bridge, of the-- Vistula bridge.
And Madritsch established a factory there.
And we walked from Plaszow to this factory every day, and walked back.
We were not as fortunate as the Schindler people, who were able to stay and live at the factory.
We had to live in the barracks.
You know that Plaszow, the camp, was made originally on the Jewish cemetery.
So they crushed the stones.
So we walked every day over people's names, you know.
So that was kind of humiliating, too-- especially to people who had, you know, relatives buried at that cemetery.
That was the largest Jewish cemetery in Krakow.
They were pretty primitive.
I mean, all we had were the barracks.
And you know, Poland gets very, very cold in the winter, so we shivered.
We were expected-- every morning, early in the morning, we were called to the appelplatz, which was a counting place where they, you know, lined us up in fives and counted us.
And we stood there for a long time, and-- you know, if the count did not jive, you had to wait until it jived before they would excuse us to go to our work.
From the time-- from the time we started going from the ghetto to the-- to the factory, we always walked in fives.
And that sort of became-- after the war, it was very difficult to walk alone.
[LAUGHS] You always looked on either side of you to see whether you had your teammates with you, or your people from your five.
But for me, that was very good, because I was a child.
And though I was tall, by being able to walk amongst other five, I was not as prominent.
So that's-- you know-- that was a security measure for me.
At the time, yes, there were quite a few children still, you know, in Plaszow.
As long as it was a labor camp.
But the one day, they had a selection in Plaszow, and they took a lot of the children away.
And some of the children hid themselves in the latrines.
You know, that's how they survived.
I remember, we smuggled a couple children out in our fives, you know, in sacks between us, out of the camp.
And then, once the camp became a concentration camp, we no longer could go out.
And so Madritsch established his factory in the camp, in some barracks, away from the living quarters.

At that point, of course, in Plaszow already, one other thing that happened when the ghetto closed is that my father, of course, then became part of the men's section, and my mother and I became part of the women's section.
And we weren't reunited again until after the war, you know.
Except at work, we saw each other at work.
But in terms of living areas, the men were the men, and the women were the women.
Well, I witnessed a couple hangings of young boys.
He happened to have sung a song or something, and they, you know, they hung him for that.
I witnessed the killing-- Goeth shooting somebody right in front of-- you know, I mean, I was in the barrack looking out, and I saw him.
He came-- he was a relative of [?
Hilowitz, ?] who was probably the most prominent kapo-- Jewish policeman in the camp.
And he came and shot this guy.
And the woman-- his wife ran out of the barrack to see what was happening, and he shot her, too.
Goeth did.
Amon Goeth, yes.
The commandant of the camp.
The commandant.
So that's one outstanding memory that I have.
Having to watch somebody being-- the hanging was terrible.
Being made to watch beatings of women, you know, that was horrible.
And always being afraid that we might be singled out for something like that, too.
The fear-- the fear was just incredible.
